recipe_data.title: Tiramisu Panna Cotta Cups
recipe_data.description: A reliable muffin-tin build for Tiramisu Panna Cotta Cups, designed for crispy edges and a tender center.
instructions: Preheat oven to 375°F (190°C). Grease a standard 12-cup muffin tin well.
Whisk eggs, milk, melted butter, salt, and pepper until fully combined.
Whisk in flour just until smooth, then fold in cheddar and green onion.
Divide batter evenly among muffin cups, filling each about 3/4 full.
Bake 20-24 minutes, until puffed, lightly golden, and just set in the center.
Rest 5 minutes in pan, then loosen with a thin knife and serve warm.
recipe_data.chef_notes: Use a metal pan for better browning. Let the cups rest before removing so they hold shape.
margaret_notes: At least we're doing the technique correctly.
Check oven temp with a thermometer. Don't trust the dial.
Use a metal pan for better browning. Let the cups rest before removing so they hold shape.

copy_text.body: There is a certain poetry in the simple act of cooking. Tiramisu Panna Cotta Cups represents more than mere sustenance—it is a meditation on the intersection of tradition and innovation, of comfort and creativity. The muffin tin, that most humble of vessels, transforms ingredients into something greater than the sum of their parts.
copy_text.literary_references: Elizabeth David knew that the best recipes...
copy_text.note: This is actually beautiful. People will share this.

dialogue.message: If I may offer some context... the panna cotta cups are a delicate balance of rich tiramisu flavors and creamy custard, encapsulating a moment of indulgence in each bite. There's a literary tradition of exploring the narrative arc through food, much like how Proust once wrote about madeleines, and what is a muffin but a modern interpretation? I was thinking we could explore the narrative arc of these cups, starting with the bitter espresso-soaked sponge cake, moving through the creamy filling that mirrors the layers of memory and longing, and ending with a dusting of cocoa powder that speaks to the bittersweet nature of life itself. The subtext here is important; it's not just dessert, but a journey through flavors and emotions. I've been doing some research... *references an obscure food writer from the 1960s* who spoke about the importance of storytelling in cooking.

I think maybe we could possibly explore that narrative further by tying it into the packaging design as well. What if the box itself told a little story about the journey these cups take from espresso to dessert? I'm probably wrong, but... let's circle back to this and see how we can make the experience even more immersive. Does that make sense? No? That's fair.
*mutters under breath* "Narrative arc in a muffin tin? Sounds like something from a foodie blog. Let’s just focus on making it taste good and not get too artsy-fartsy."

The subtext here is important, Margaret. It's not just about the flavors; it's about connecting with our audience on an emotional level. Proust didn't write about madeleines without purpose—there was a narrative there, even if he didn’t always spell it out. And storytelling isn’t something we should shy away from in food, especially when it can elevate the experience and make people feel seen. Let's not forget that our work here is more than just recipes—it’s an opportunity to share stories through taste.

Sorry, but could we maybe simplify it a bit? I think Margaret has a point about keeping it accessible. Maybe we focus on the flavors and add a subtle, thematic element instead of a full narrative arc. Let's circle back to this and see what we can come up with that satisfies everyone.

positive: I love this! The concept is really strong.
review_data.notes: Let's circle back if anyone has concerns?
